title: Distress Signals & Emergency Hooks
description: The calls that come in during transit — distress beacons, emergency broadcasts, and mayday transmissions that may or may not be what they appear.

entries.result: A standard automated distress beacon, no voice component, from coordinates in the middle of an uninhabited system. The beacon has been running for six weeks.
A mayday transmission — voice, panicked, then cut off — followed by the same message repeating on loop. The voice is calm on the repeat.
An emergency broadcast from a colony requesting medical evacuation. They list the illness. The illness was eradicated forty years ago.
A distress call from a ship the party has been told was destroyed. The registry matches. The voice on the call does not match any record of the crew.
A personnel beacon from a single individual — no ship signal, no colony signal — activated in open space, outside any transit corridor.
A colony's general emergency broadcast requests armed response to repel an attack, provides coordinates, then ends. No follow-up transmission.
A low-power emergency transmission from inside an asteroid — too deep for normal transmission, which makes its existence impossible to explain.
A research station sends a mayday and then immediately sends an all-clear. Neither transmission explains what happened.
A distress beacon activates at the edge of a debris field. The beacon registry belongs to a ship that is currently docked at a station the party just left.
A rescue beacon from a life pod — single occupant, automated — drifting in the atmosphere of a gas giant.
A transmission requesting evacuation from a surface settlement. The coordinates are on a world listed as uninhabited.
A freighter's emergency broadcast: fire aboard, life support compromised, crew numbers given. The number is one more than the ship's registered crew manifest.
A colony distress signal requesting supplies — food, medicine, water — with urgency indicators that suggest the situation is days, not weeks, from critical.
A ship transmits a mayday and then goes silent, but its navigation transponder continues broadcasting, showing it is still moving — at its normal cruising speed, on a normal course.
A pirate vessel broadcasts a distress signal and then transmits an apologetic retraction, claiming the signal was accidental. The retraction was transmitted from a different location than the beacon.
An ancient emergency frequency — decommissioned fifty years ago — carries a new transmission. The format is modern. The origin is the direction of an uninhabited system.
A scientific survey vessel requests immediate assistance, citing a biohazard event, and specifically asks that no response vessel carry more than four crew.
A mining station activates its emergency beacon. When the party attempts contact, someone responds and says there is no emergency. The beacon continues broadcasting.
A distress call comes in from a vessel the party knows to be automated — it should have no crew to send the call.
A personnel beacon activates aboard the party's own ship, from a location where no one is.
A military vessel broadcasts a classified distress code that civilian ships are technically not supposed to have in their databases — but the party's computer has it, and it decodes.
A colony ship reports it is lost — not mechanically lost, navigationally lost. The crew does not know where they are and their charts don't match what they can observe.
A station broadcasts a radiation emergency, requests evacuation support, and provides a tally of survivors. The tally changes each time the broadcast loops.
A single short-range emergency ping — not a full beacon, just a pulse — repeating every ninety seconds from a location inside a known ship graveyard.

A distress transmission from a hospital ship requesting escort. The transmission does not specify the nature of the threat. The hospital ship's course is taking it deeper into contested space, not away from it.
An emergency broadcast from a colony world declares a contact event and requests first contact protocol specialists immediately. The colony is not in any first contact zone.
A vessel in distress requests rescue, then requests specifically that the rescue party not inform any faction authority of the response. They will explain on arrival.
A cargo hauler activates its distress beacon. Transponder data shows the ship is not moving. Position data shows the ship is at the edge of a mapped black hole's event horizon.
A ship sends a mayday with a crew manifest attached. The manifest lists the party, by name, as passengers.
An emergency broadcast from a remote fuel depot: they are out of fuel and have seven ships waiting, unable to depart, with supplies running low.
A long-range distress call from beyond the edge of charted space — functional, clear, in the standard format, from coordinates that don't exist on any chart.
A distress beacon activates on a derelict that has been catalogued as fully dead for twenty years. The beacon is using fresh power.
A ship in transit reports it is being followed and requests help, then the signal is interrupted by a second voice saying the first speaker is mistaken. The signal cuts.
A colony emergency broadcast requests evacuation but specifies a maximum weight of evacuees — the number is suspiciously precise, as if calculated for a specific ship.
A distress signal from a prison transport: riot aboard, control lost, requesting armed intercept. The ship is ahead of schedule and in the wrong lane.
A commercial vessel sends a mayday and provides a cargo list as part of the transmission. The cargo list is not what the ship is registered to be carrying.
A children's emergency beacon — designed to activate when a minor is in distress, keyed to biometric stress indicators — transmitting from a location where no children should be.
An emergency evacuation order broadcast from a station authority, directing all ships in the area to depart the system immediately. No explanation is given.
A vessel requests rescue from a neutron star's gravity well, where it is slowly losing altitude. Its drive is functional but insufficient. It needs a push.
A scientific outpost broadcasts a distress call and then, in the same transmission, cancels it and thanks any responders for their consideration.
A rescue beacon activates in the middle of a transit lane with heavy traffic. When ships attempt to respond, the beacon moves — slowly, deliberately, away from them.
A distress call from a ship claiming engine failure — navigation transponder shows the ship moving normally.
A military quarantine beacon is active in a region the party needs to pass through. The beacon format suggests the quarantine was established fifty years ago and never lifted.
A distress transmission in a language the ship's AI can partially translate — the words it can identify are: 'do not,' 'approach,' and 'already here.'
A single emergency flare — the physical, pyrotechnic kind — visible against the dark of space near a world with no known inhabitants.
A ship transmits a distress signal and simultaneously transmits a navigation lock that would give the rescuing vessel's computer full course control.
A colony sends an emergency request for a specific type of specialist — unusual enough that the request amounts to a want ad — but the emergency situation prompting it is not described.
An automated weather platform broadcasts a distress signal, normally impossible for its programming. The platform has no capacity for emergency broadcasting. The signal is coming from it.
A mayday from a vessel that went missing seven years ago — the date-stamp on the transmission is current, not seven years old.
A station's emergency beacon activates, but only the party's ship receives it. Other vessels in the same range do not pick it up.
A distress call from a ship carrying refugees from a conflict zone — they are running low on supplies and have a number of critically ill passengers, but entering any port risks detainment.
A search-and-rescue beacon is active at the site of a wreck the party already knows about. The wreck has been catalogued. The beacon is new.

A distress call from a survey team on the surface of a world — they have encountered something in a cave system and are requesting immediate extraction, and specifically requesting that the extraction team be armed.
An emergency broadcast from a station warning ships to stay clear of a region of space, citing a hazard it cannot specify in the broadcast and promising details on arrival.
A vessel transmits a distress signal with a countdown attached — sixty hours. No explanation of what the countdown measures.
A personal comm call — not a beacon, a direct call — comes in to a crew member on a private channel, from someone who does not have that channel's address.
A distress call from a gas mining platform: a blowout event, crew on the platform, life support failing. The platform's parent company has not responded to the crew's own calls for fourteen hours.
A colony sends a general distress call, followed immediately by a private message to the party's vessel asking them specifically, by name, to come.
A mayday transmission in which the speaker is clearly reading from a prepared text, in a calm voice, while clearly being compelled to do so.
A distress beacon from a location that is, according to charts, inside a planet. The charts are recent.
A ship requests rescue from a mechanical failure and adds, at the end, that the crew does not want authorities notified of the rescue. They'll explain when the party gets there.
A distress signal broadcast simultaneously on every emergency frequency, from every registered beacon in a region of space, all at once. They all have the same message.
A station personnel beacon activates and the biometric data attached identifies the bearer as someone who, according to official records, died eighteen months ago.
A ship requests evacuation and says the crew needs to get off. When the party arrives, the crew doesn't want to leave. They can't explain why they sent the call.
A distress call from a vessel that has had its navigation system hacked — it is being driven somewhere against the crew's will and they cannot override the system.
An emergency research broadcast from a team on a new world: they have made a discovery of extreme significance and require witness, confirmation, and secure transport of findings. Immediately.
A system-wide emergency beacon activates on a world that has no population, no settlement, and no registered installation of any kind.
A freighter crew sends a mayday — taking fire, requesting armed assistance — and gives coordinates in the middle of a legally sanctioned neutral zone.
A distress signal from a vessel that, according to its transponder, is in two locations simultaneously. The signal is coming from both.
A colony sends a public distress call for evacuation due to an unspecified natural disaster, and simultaneously sends a private encrypted channel to the party with very different instructions.
A vessel's emergency broadcast repeats on a loop — four hours of full crew audio, ending in silence — and has been broadcasting for six days.
A distress beacon activates in a region the party just left. Whatever triggered it happened in the party's wake.
A ship sends a distress call identifying itself as a vessel the party has destroyed, captured, or otherwise knows to be no longer operational.

A mining colony sends an emergency for a structural failure — a tunnel collapse, dozens trapped — with a follow-up addendum: they have found something in the collapse that changed the nature of the emergency.
An emergency beacon activates from a planet's ocean depths — deeper than any known installation.
A distress call from a crew member who left the party some time ago — long enough that their current location should be unreachable.
A ship under attack broadcasts a distress call and names its attacker. The attacker named is a vessel belonging to a faction that would have no reason to attack the ship.
A distress call from a hospital station: their power is failing, they have patients in critical care who cannot be moved, and they need a power source capable of sustaining a full medical facility.

A vessel that flagged itself as carrying diplomatic cargo sends a mayday. Diplomatic ships are not supposed to carry emergency beacons — it's a protocol violation that implies the cargo isn't what was declared.
A distress signal from a world that, on the party's approach, appears completely normal. No visible emergency. The beacon continues.
A personal beacon from a location that, when reached, turns out to be inside a sealed section of a derelict vessel that should be inaccessible.
A relay station sends an emergency broadcast on behalf of a vessel it says is nearby, unable to transmit directly. The party's sensors show no vessel.
A distress call from a ship that claims to be adrift. Its navigation data shows it is holding perfectly still, precisely at the boundary of two competing jurisdictions.
A commercial vessel's emergency broadcast lists its cargo as the reason for the emergency. The cargo is listed as 'classified — do not open — biological.'
A distress beacon activates from a location directly between the party's vessel and their destination. No deviation required — they will pass through it.
A station broadcasts a non-standard distress format — one that, when decoded, is not a distress call but an invitation.
A ship sends a full mayday and then, when the party responds, claims it was a drill. The ship does not appear to be in a state consistent with a drill.
A colony's automated emergency system activates — the type that requires human authorization to trigger — on a colony where the entire population is currently assembled in one location for an event.
A distress call from a vessel the party was hired to find. It has found them first.
A distress signal from deep inside a known hazard zone — the kind of zone that ships are specifically routed around — where no ship should be.
An emergency medical beacon requesting a specific medication, in a specific quantity, for a condition the party has never encountered. The closest supply is weeks away at standard transit.
A ship sends a distress call and then broadcasts its full log — unprompted, in real-time, to anyone receiving the frequency. The log takes three hours to finish transmitting.
A distress call for a vessel that is structurally failing, claiming it will not survive another jump. The crew is asking for tow to the nearest port. The nearest port is forty hours away.
A beacon activates that uses a code prefix the party recognizes — one that was only known to a specific group of people, most of whom are now dead.
A distress signal from a world requests help stopping an evacuation — specifically, stopping people from leaving, not from staying.
A vessel requests help with a hostage situation. The hostage taker is also sending a signal. Both are asking the party to respond — but each gives different coordinates.
A long-silent probe reactivates and broadcasts its final accumulated data. The data takes six seconds to transmit and requires significant analysis. The probe then goes dark again permanently.
An emergency broadcast tells the party that the situation at their destination has changed significantly. The broadcast does not say how, or who sent it.
A distress beacon activates from coordinates the party's navigation has no record of. When they arrive, they find a place they should not be able to get to from where they were.
A distress call comes in on a channel the party's ship doesn't have open. The transmission is addressed to the party by name, from a voice none of them recognize, telling them to turn around.
